cards.front: What is offshore surveying?
cards.back: The work of mapping what is under the water -- the seabed, structures on it, and conditions around them -- turning the underwater environment into something measurable and actionable.

cards.front: What is the role of a Party Chief / Offshore Manager?
cards.back: Runs the survey operation. Sets priorities, coordinates between the vessel, the client, and the survey team. The first point of contact when you are unsure about something.

cards.front: What does a Data Processor do?
cards.back: Takes raw survey data, applies corrections, cleans it up, and produces deliverables -- charts, 3D models, and reports. Works closely with surveyors to ensure accuracy.

cards.front: What do Geophysicists and Geologists do on a survey project?
cards.back: They interpret survey data to determine what the seafloor is made of, what lies beneath the sediment, and why a particular area looks the way it does.

cards.front: What does HSE stand for?
cards.back: Health, Safety, and Environment. HSE officers keep everyone safe and the environment protected.

cards.front: What is the bow of a vessel?
cards.back: The front of the vessel.

cards.front: What is the stern of a vessel?
cards.back: The rear of the vessel.

cards.front: How do you remember which side is port?
cards.back: 'Port' and 'left' both have four letters. Port is the left side when facing forward.

cards.front: What is the bridge on a vessel?
cards.back: The vessel's command center where navigation and overall control happen.

cards.front: What is the galley?
cards.back: The kitchen and dining area on a vessel, sometimes called the mess.

cards.front: What is a muster station?
cards.back: Your designated meeting point during drills or emergencies. Find it the moment you board.

cards.front: What does 'online team' mean on a survey vessel?
cards.back: Surveyors managing live, real-time operations -- monitoring positioning, running data acquisition, managing equipment, and communicating with bridge and ROV teams.

cards.front: What does 'offline team' mean on a survey vessel?
cards.back: Data processors, geophysicists, and specialists who work on data after acquisition -- cleaning, processing, QC, and preparing deliverables.

cards.front: What is the standard radio call-and-response format?
cards.back: Identify yourself and who you are calling, wait for acknowledgment, state your message, receive confirmation. Example: 'Bridge, Survey.' -- 'Bridge, go ahead.' -- State request -- 'Copy.'

cards.front: What are the typical shift patterns offshore?
cards.back: 12-hour shifts. Consistent sleep, eating well, staying hydrated, and using downtime to recharge are key to managing the routine.

cards.front: What are the five essential PPE items on deck?
cards.back: Hard hat, steel-toed boots, high-visibility vest/overalls, gloves, and safety glasses.

cards.front: What should you do first when boarding a vessel regarding safety?
cards.back: Find your muster station immediately. Know how to get there from anywhere on the vessel.

cards.front: What is MARPOL?
cards.back: The Marine Pollution Convention -- international rules to prevent pollution from ships.

cards.front: What is IMCA?
cards.back: International Marine Contractors Association -- provides guidance on marine contracting, including survey and positioning practices.

cards.front: What is IHO?
cards.back: International Hydrographic Organization -- sets standards for hydrographic surveying and nautical charting.

cards.front: What are the three key environmental responsibilities for offshore surveyors?
cards.back: Minimize disturbance (manage noise levels), proper waste management (nothing goes overboard), and report wildlife sightings.

cards.front: What is an oblate spheroid?
cards.back: Earth's approximate shape -- fatter at the equator and slightly flattened at the poles.

cards.front: What is the geoid?
cards.back: The gravity-defined shape of the Earth's actual surface, reflecting variations in gravity and mass distribution.

cards.front: What is an ellipsoid in surveying?
cards.back: A mathematical model that approximates Earth's shape, smoothing out irregularities into a computable surface.

cards.front: What is a datum?
cards.back: A reference framework for measurements. It defines the origin, orientation, and scale for a coordinate system. WGS84 is the most common global datum.

cards.front: What is WGS84?
cards.back: World Geodetic System 1984 -- the most common global datum, used by GPS.

cards.front: What is UTM?
cards.back: Universal Transverse Mercator -- a projection that divides the world into 60 zones, each 6 degrees wide, providing metric coordinates (eastings and northings) for survey work.

cards.front: What happens if you use the wrong datum?
cards.back: All positions are offset from reality. Mixing up WGS84 and a local datum can introduce errors of tens or even hundreds of meters.

cards.front: What is the difference between geographic and projected coordinates?
cards.back: Geographic coordinates use latitude and longitude in degrees (global references). Projected coordinates use eastings and northings in meters (day-to-day survey work).

cards.front: What four GNSS constellations are in operation?
cards.back: GPS (U.S.), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (Europe), and BeiDou (China).

cards.front: What accuracy does standard GNSS provide?
cards.back: Within a few meters.

cards.front: What is RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)?
cards.back: A GNSS augmentation technique that uses a base station on a known point to broadcast corrections, achieving centimeter-level positioning accuracy.

cards.front: What is an INS (Inertial Navigation System)?
cards.back: A system using accelerometers and gyroscopes to track movement independently of external signals. Bridges GNSS outages but drifts over time without corrections.

cards.front: How does USBL positioning work?
cards.back: A vessel-mounted transducer sends an acoustic pulse. A beacon on the ROV or subsea equipment replies. The system measures the angle and time delay to calculate the beacon's 3D position.

cards.front: How does LBL positioning work?
cards.back: Multiple transponders on the seabed create an underwater reference network. Position is triangulated by measuring ranges to each transponder.

cards.front: When is LBL preferred over USBL?
cards.back: For permanent or high-precision operations like deepwater drilling and long-term construction campaigns where centimeter-level accuracy is needed and USBL alone cannot deliver it.

cards.front: What does a DVL (Doppler Velocity Log) measure?
cards.back: Speed and direction of movement relative to the seabed, by sending acoustic beams and measuring the Doppler shift of return signals.

cards.front: What does an MRU (Motion Reference Unit) track?
cards.back: Pitch, roll, and heave -- the motion a vessel or ROV experiences. Essential for compensating sensor data from systems like multibeam echo sounders.

cards.front: What three factors affect sound velocity in water?
cards.back: Temperature, salinity, and depth (pressure).

cards.front: What is an SVP (Sound Velocity Profile)?
cards.back: A measurement of the speed of sound at various depths through the water column, obtained by lowering an SV probe. Used to correct acoustic data.

cards.front: Why do you need regular SVP updates?
cards.back: Water column conditions change over time. Skipping sound velocity corrections leads to warped or misaligned survey data from acoustic systems.

cards.front: What is an SBES (Single Beam Echo Sounder)?
cards.back: Sends a single acoustic pulse straight down to measure depth at one point beneath the vessel. Simple and reliable but provides no wide coverage.

cards.front: What is an MBES (Multibeam Echo Sounder)?
cards.back: Emits multiple beams across a fan-shaped swath, mapping a broad area of the seafloor in one pass. Produces detailed 3D models of the seabed.

cards.front: What does Side Scan Sonar (SSS) do?
cards.back: Produces near-photographic grayscale imagery of the seabed texture by recording the intensity of returning acoustic echoes. Excels at detecting objects like pipelines, debris, wrecks, and boulders.

cards.front: What does a Sub-Bottom Profiler (SBP) do?
cards.back: Emits low-frequency pulses that penetrate the seabed, revealing layered reflections of the subsurface -- soft mud, sandy sediment, bedrock.

cards.front: What do magnetometers and gradiometers detect?
cards.back: Variations in Earth's magnetic field caused by ferrous (iron-containing) objects -- old anchors, abandoned pipelines, unexploded ordnance.

cards.front: What is LIDAR used for in surveying?
cards.back: Light Detection and Ranging -- uses lasers for highly accurate surface models, typically of coastal areas or structures partly above the waterline. Limited to shallow depths because light attenuates quickly in water.

cards.front: What is an ROV?
cards.back: Remotely Operated Vehicle -- a submersible robot connected to the surface by a tether carrying power and communications, piloted with real-time video and sensor feedback.

cards.front: What is an AUV?
cards.back: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle -- operates without a tether, pre-programmed with a mission plan, navigates and collects data independently.

cards.front: What is an ROTV?
cards.back: Remotely Operated Towed Vehicle -- towed behind the vessel with control surfaces for adjusting depth and attitude. Typically used for towing side scan sonar or magnetometer arrays.

cards.front: What is the TMS on an ROV system?
cards.back: Tether Management System -- a cage-like frame between the umbilical and the ROV that houses a length of ROV tether and manages its payout. Lowered to working depth on the main umbilical.

cards.front: What is the splash zone and why is it dangerous?
cards.back: The region at the water surface where wave energy is highest. The most dangerous phase of ROV operations is transit through the splash zone during launch and recovery.

cards.front: What is the difference between online and offline QC?
cards.back: Online QC monitors data in real time during acquisition, catching errors immediately. Offline QC cleans data after acquisition, applies advanced corrections, and flags anomalies for the final deliverables.

cards.front: What five elements does a typical survey plan cover?
cards.back: Scope and objectives, equipment selection, line planning, timing and weather windows, and coordination between bridge, deck crew, ROV team, and surveyors.

cards.front: What is a route survey?
cards.back: A survey that maps and assesses a planned path for cables, pipelines, or other subsea infrastructure. Typically combines MBES, SSS, magnetometers, and sub-bottom profilers.

cards.front: What tools are used in a geotechnical investigation?
cards.back: Cone penetration tests (CPTs), vibrocoring, and box coring -- tools that physically sample the soil to determine seabed composition and bearing capacity.

cards.front: What is the purpose of environmental baseline surveys?
cards.back: Conducted before major offshore projects to sample sediments, map benthic habitats, and check water quality, confirming the project won't damage sensitive zones.

cards.front: What software is Qinsy used for?
cards.back: Real-time survey data acquisition. It handles live data logging and monitoring during survey operations.

cards.front: What software is Qimera used for?
cards.back: Post-processing and visualization of bathymetric data. It is the companion software to Qinsy.

cards.front: What is SIS (Seafloor Information System)?
cards.back: Software paired with Kongsberg multibeam systems for real-time visualization and data quality checks.

cards.front: What is Survey Manager in Qinsy?
cards.back: The project setup module where you define the vessel, sensors, offsets, and coordinate systems -- everything needed before data acquisition begins.

cards.front: What is the difference between CAD and GIS in survey work?
cards.back: CAD produces precise engineering drawings of structures, routes, or layouts. GIS layers and analyzes spatial data, showing relationships between survey results and other geographical information.

cards.front: Why does calibration matter?
cards.back: Calibration fine-tunes equipment so data is accurate and trustworthy. Even slight instrument errors propagate through the entire dataset, leading to costly mistakes.

cards.front: What is a USBL box-in test?
cards.back: A calibration procedure for USBL systems where the vessel maneuvers around a transponder in a box pattern to identify systematic errors in the acoustic positioning.

cards.front: What is an MBES patch test?
cards.back: A calibration procedure that determines angular offsets (pitch, roll, heading) between the multibeam transducer and the vessel's motion sensor by running specific survey line patterns.

cards.front: What is the difference between calibration and verification?
cards.back: Calibration is about getting measurements right. Verification is about confirming they stay right under real-world conditions.

cards.front: What three types of calibration documents will you work with?
cards.back: Project Procedure Documents (job-specific instructions), Manufacturer Manuals (recommended intervals and methods), and Industry Standards (general best practices from bodies like IMCA).

cards.front: What are the benefits of remote surveying?
cards.back: Lower travel costs, 24/7 collaboration across time zones, fewer people exposed to offshore hazards, and reduced carbon footprint from fewer crew transfers.

cards.front: What is a Remote Operations Center (ROC)?
cards.back: An onshore facility with large monitors displaying live vessel positions, sensor readouts, and real-time communications, allowing survey teams to monitor operations without being on the vessel.

cards.front: What advantage does Starlink offer over traditional VSAT?
cards.back: Starlink is a LEO constellation offering high speed and low latency, compared to geostationary VSAT which has 500-750 ms latency. It enables near real-time remote processing and troubleshooting.

cards.front: What is the latency of Iridium satellite communications?
cards.back: About 50 ms, much lower than geostationary VSAT. Iridium uses 66 LEO satellites providing truly global coverage including polar regions.

cards.front: What VSAT frequency band is most common on offshore vessels?
cards.back: Ku-Band (12-18 GHz) -- good bandwidth at reasonable cost, but degrades in heavy rain (rain fade).

cards.front: What is a DSV (Diving Support Vessel)?
cards.back: A vessel designed to put divers in the water safely, carrying saturation diving systems -- pressurised chambers, diving bells, and life support. Requires DP2 or DP3.

cards.front: What is an IMR vessel?
cards.back: Inspection, Maintenance and Repair vessel -- the workhorse of subsea operations, set up for well intervention, maintenance, and general subsea work with ROVs. Typically DP2.

cards.front: What is a CSV (Construction Support Vessel)?
cards.back: A heavy-lift vessel with large deck areas and significant crane capacity (sometimes hundreds of tonnes) for installing subsea structures, manifolds, spool pieces, and umbilicals.

cards.front: What is an AHTS vessel?
cards.back: Anchor Handling Tug Supply Vessel -- handles anchors and mooring chains for drilling rigs, tows rigs into position, deploys and retrieves anchors. Has high bollard pull and stern rollers.

cards.front: What is Dynamic Positioning (DP)?
cards.back: A computer-controlled system that maintains a vessel's position and heading using its own thrusters, without anchors, by processing inputs from GNSS, acoustic systems, environmental sensors, and MRUs.

cards.front: What inputs does a DP system use?
cards.back: Position reference sensors (GNSS, acoustic, taut wire), environmental sensors (wind, current), motion sensors (MRU), and control computers that calculate thruster demands.

cards.front: What is a moonpool?
cards.back: A vertical opening through the hull, open to the sea below. Equipment deployed through it is sheltered from surface wave action, enabling operations in higher sea states.

cards.front: What is an A-frame on a vessel?
cards.back: A steel frame shaped like the letter A, usually at the stern, that swings out over the water to deploy and recover equipment like ROVs and towed sonar fish. Typical capacity 5-25 tonnes.

cards.front: What is a LARS?
cards.back: Launch and Recovery System -- the complete assembly for getting an ROV into and out of the water, including the A-frame/gantry, winch, TMS, cursor/docking head, and heave compensation.

cards.front: What is VSAT?
cards.back: Very Small Aperture Terminal -- the primary broadband satellite communication system on most offshore vessels, using a stabilised dish antenna tracking geostationary satellites.

cards.front: Why are raw multibeam datasets often shipped on hard drives rather than transferred via satellite?
cards.back: Raw multibeam datasets are often too large to transfer over satellite links, even with VSAT. Physical hard drive shipment remains practical for very large files.

cards.front: What five basic IT troubleshooting steps should you follow?
cards.back: 1) Restart software, 2) Check cable connections, 3) Verify licenses and system resources, 4) Close unneeded tasks, 5) Document what you tried.

cards.front: What are three cybersecurity basics for offshore workers?
cards.back: Avoid suspicious emails (phishing), never share passwords, and report anything odd (strange pop-ups, suspicious external drives).

cards.front: What is the most useful application of AI tools for offshore surveyors?
cards.back: Automating routine tasks through scripting (file renaming, data parsing, batch conversion), drafting reports, brainstorming and problem-solving, and quick concept refreshers.

cards.front: What is the key rule when using AI for critical survey work?
cards.back: Always verify AI outputs against project documentation and human expertise. Never feed confidential project information into public AI systems.

cards.front: What three communication skills matter most offshore?
cards.back: Active listening, adapting tone and detail to your audience, and maintaining respectful language even under pressure.

cards.front: Why is time management important on a survey vessel?
cards.back: Long shifts, unpredictable weather, and multiple competing tasks require structured time management. Good handover documentation at shift/rotation end helps the next person start smoothly.

cards.front: What certifications are relevant for offshore surveyors?
cards.back: IMCA survey competence scheme, Cat A/Cat B hydrographic surveyor certification (FIG/IHO), manufacturer-specific training (Kongsberg, Sonardyne, Exail), and HSE certifications (BOSIET, HUET, offshore medical).

cards.front: What coping strategies help with mental health offshore?
cards.back: Getting out on deck during breaks, keeping in touch with people at home, maintaining a routine outside work hours, exercising, eating well, and reaching out for help when needed.

cards.front: What is DP assurance governed by?
cards.back: IMCA M 191, which sets out annual DP trial requirements. DP2 and DP3 vessels undergo FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) verification.

cards.front: What is the BUC in a VSAT system?
cards.back: Block Upconverter -- converts signal frequencies for uplink transmission. Higher wattage (8W, 16W, 25W) means faster upload speeds.
